summary: A 1914-1918 global conflict, fought primarily between the Allied Powers (Britain, France, Russia, and later the United States and Italy) and the Central Powers (Germany, Austria-Hungary, the Ottoman Empire, and Bulgaria), that began with the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo and the collapse of Europe's alliance system into general war. It is identified in clues by the poetry of Wilfred Owen ("Dulce et Decorum Est," "Anthem for Doomed Youth"), the stalemate of trench warfare on the Western Front, the punitive "war guilt" clause of the Treaty of Versailles, and its status as the setting for works like All Quiet on the Western Front and A Farewell to Arms.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Giveaway-level clues identify this war through its association with Wilfred Owen's war poetry, its ending in the punitive Treaty of Versailles, the propaganda and recruitment culture surrounding it (white feathers, recruiting posters, music-hall songs), and the derogatory wartime terms used for Germany. It is the conflict most reliably clued via Owen, trench warfare, and the phrase "war guilt."

clues.clue: Wilfred Owen wrote poems including "Dulce et Decorum Est," "Anthem for Doomed Youth," and "Strange Meeting" during this war and was killed one week before its 1918 armistice
examples: name this conflict, the setting of "Dulce et decorum est" by Wilfred Owen.
Name this war. Wilfred Owen wrote about it until his death one week before its 1918 armistice.

clues.clue: The treaty that ended this war (the Treaty of Versailles) assigned blame to the loser in its "war guilt" clause, analyzed critically by John Maynard Keynes in The Economic Consequences of the Peace
examples: name this event whose loser was assigned responsibility in the war guilt clause of the Treaty of Versailles.
The treaty that ended this war is analyzed in The Economic Consequences of the Peace, which analyzes its punitive "war guilt" clause.

clues.clue: This war inspired classical works including Benjamin Britten's War Requiem (which sets Owen's poems) and Maurice Ravel's Le Tombeau de Couperin, whose six movements are each dedicated to a friend who died in it
examples: name this conflict that inspired Benjamin Britten's War Requiem.
Six movements dedicated to participants in this event make up Le tombeau de Couperin.

clues.clue: Women handed out white feathers to men not in uniform to shame them into enlisting
examples: To shame men into enlisting into the army during this war, women handed out white feathers to young men not in uniform.

clues.clue: Recruiting posters included a girl asking her father "Daddy, what did YOU do in the war?" and the "Lord Kitchener Wants You" poster; the side wearing spiked helmets was denounced as "huns," and zeppelin bombing raids were called "baby-killers"
examples: A poster from this war depicts a girl asking her father "Daddy, what did YOU do in [this war]?"
The term "baby-killers" was used during this war for zeppelins used in bombing raids.

clues.clue: Recruiting music-hall songs of this war included "Keep the Home Fires Burning" (composed by Ivor Novello), George M. Cohan's "Over There," and "It's a Long Way to Tipperary"
examples: Name this conflict for which Ivor Novello composed the song "Keep the Home Fires Burning." George M. Cohen's "Over There" enjoins Americans to fight in this war against the "Hun."
Music hall performers like Vesta Tilley helped recruit many English soldiers for this war by singing popular songs such as "Keep the Home Fires Burning" and "It's a Long Way to Tipperary."

clues.clue: Rupert Brooke's poem "The Soldier" ("some corner of a foreign field / That is for ever England") was published during this war
examples: Name this poem that begins, "If I should die, think this of me: That there's some corner of a foreign field That is for ever England."

works.name: Causes and Outbreak
works.description: This war began in the summer of 1914 after the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand collapsed Europe's system of alliances into general war, with Germany's Schlieffen Plan and violation of Belgian neutrality bringing Britain in against it. Clues about its origins lean heavily on historiography, citing a long tradition of scholars who have debated whether the war was caused by German aggression, mutual miscalculation, or domestic political pressures.

clues.clue: The war began with the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand by the Black Hand in Sarajevo
examples: name this war that began with the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand.
The battles of Vimy Ridge and the Somme occurred during this war, which began after Archduke Franz Ferdinand was assassinated by the Black Hand in Sarajevo.

clues.clue: The Triple Entente (Britain, France, and Russia) entered the war in 1914; Britain in particular declared war after Germany, executing the Schlieffen Plan, violated a treaty guaranteeing Belgian neutrality that was derided as a "scrap of paper"
examples: An agreement derided as a "scrap of paper" caused Britain to take this action over a violation of Belgian neutrality due to the Schlieffen Plan.
name this action taken by the Triple Entente in 1914 to address German aggression.

clues.clue: Germany's war aims for territorial expansion were laid out in the Septemberprogramm
examples: This war's loser had originally planned territorial expansion with its septemberprogramm.
Luigi Albertini's thesis about this event was challenged by Fritz Fischer, who used the Septemberprogramm as evidence of goals in it.

clues.clue: The origins of the war have been debated by historians: A.J.P. Taylor's War by Timetable proposed the "Railway Thesis," arguing mobilization made the war unstoppable once begun; Barbara Tuchman's The Guns of August covered the pre-war "Willy-Nicky" letters between the Kaiser and the Tsar; and Christopher Clark's The Sleepwalkers rejected traditional explanations of blame
examples: Taylor proposed the "Railway Thesis" of this war's origins in his book War by Timetable, in which he argued that this war could not be stopped after mobilization began.
Willy-Nicky letters were exchanged in the run-up to this war, whose origins were chronicled by Barbara Tuchman in The Guns of August.
Traditional explanations of the cause of this event were rejected in Christopher Clark's The Sleepwalkers.

clues.clue: Fritz Fischer controversially argued Germany bore primary responsibility for the war (the "Fischer School/Controversy," analyzed by John Moses), while Arno Mayer instead attributed the war to the aristocracy's "Primacy of Domestic Politics"
examples: John Moses wrote a book about the "Controversy" surrounding the theories on the origins of this war by German historian Fritz Fischer.
Arno Mayer argued that this event's cause was less foreign policy and more the aristocracy's attempt to maintain domestic power.

clues.clue: Helmuth von Moltke the Younger led the German General Staff at the war's outset and implemented the Schlieffen Plan before suffering a nervous breakdown and retiring after the First Battle of the Marne
examples: Helmuth von Moltke the Younger led the General Staff early in this war, where he implemented the Schlieffen Plan. He had a nervous breakdown and retired after this war's First Battle of the Marne.

works.name: The Western Front
works.description: The Western Front settled into trench warfare after the German advance was halted, and it became the war's most heavily mythologized theater in literature and memory. Clues describing it draw on both military detail (the Marne, Passchendaele) and the cultural memory of trench life, including the Christmas Truce and the poppy-covered fields of Flanders.

clues.clue: Dead soldiers are commemorated with "crosses, row on row" in John McCrae's poem "In Flanders Fields" ("In Flanders fields, the poppies blow / Between the crosses, row on row")
examples: Canadian schoolchildren often recite a poem written during this event that begins "In Flanders fields, the poppies blow / Between the crosses, row on row."

clues.clue: British and German soldiers legendarily played soccer during the Christmas Truce of 1914
examples: Name this war during which English and German soldiers legendarily played soccer during the Christmas Truce of 1914.

clues.clue: The German advance was stopped at the First Battle of the Marne early in the war
examples: He had a nervous breakdown and retired after this war's First Battle of the Marne.

clues.clue: The Canadian Corps helped turn the tide at the Battle of Passchendaele and spearheaded the war's Hundred Days Offensive
examples: The conscription crisis of 1917 arose during this war, in which the Canadian Corps helped turn the tide at Passchendaele. Canadian troops spearheaded this war's Hundred Days Offensive.

clues.clue: The Battle of the Somme and the Battle of Vimy Ridge were fought on this front
examples: The battles of Vimy Ridge and the Somme occurred during this war
Bourassa and Laurier agreed in opposing Robert Borden's conscription measures during this war, which saw numerous Canadians killed at the Battle of the Somme.

works.name: The Eastern Front
works.description: The Eastern Front pitted Russia against Germany and Austria-Hungary and was marked by early Russian disasters, a long and costly siege, and Russia's eventual collapse and withdrawal. Clues about it cluster around the opening battles of Tannenberg and the Masurian Lakes and the war-ending Treaty of Brest-Litovsk.

clues.clue: Germany's fast railway-enabled mobility led to the Russian Second Army's decisive defeat at the Battle of Tannenberg; the shamed Russian general Alexander Samsonov committed suicide after the defeat
examples: After suffering a major defeat at the start of this war, ashamed general Alexander Samsonov commited suicide.
Fast movement enabled by German railways during this war led to the Russian Second Army's decisive defeat at Tannenberg.

clues.clue: General Paul von Rennenkampf lost the first of two Battles of the Masurian Lakes
examples: During this war, the first of two battles near the Masurian Lakes was lost by general Paul von Rennenkampf.
an early battle in this war ironically saw a general named Paul von Rennenkampf lose to a German army.

clues.clue: General Aleksei Brusilov launched an offensive (the Brusilov Offensive) that was one of the war's deadliest operations
examples: The Brusilov Offensive was one of this war's deadliest operations, and the slogan "peace, land, and bread" was coined in opposition to this war.
General Alexsei Brusilov launched a failed offensive during this war.

clues.clue: The Gorlice-Tarnow Offensive led to the "Great Retreat," leaving Galicia under enemy control and prompting the Tsar to personally take command of the army; the six-month siege of Przemysl saw brutal conditions
examples: The so-called "Great Retreat" followed this war's Gorlice-Tarnow Offensive, which ended with one side in firm control of Galicia and caused the Tsar to personally take control of the army.
This conflict saw brutal conditions during the six month siege of Przemysl

clues.clue: Russia exited the war via the Treaty of Brest-Litovsk (1917-18) amid the Russian Revolution, ceding most of Ukraine, which was regained a year later after the failure of Germany's Spring Offensive
examples: name this war which Russia exited by signing the Treaty of Brest-Litovsk in the midst of the Russian Revolution.
Most of Ukraine was ceded by a treaty annulled a year later after the failure of this war's Spring Offensive.

works.name: The Gallipoli Campaign and the Middle Eastern Theater
works.description: This war extended into the Ottoman Empire's territory through the failed Gallipoli Campaign, which shaped Australian and New Zealand national identity, and the Arab Revolt against Ottoman rule, which is closely associated with T.E. Lawrence. Together these campaigns supplied much of the war's clueing outside the Western Front.

clues.clue: The Gallipoli Campaign was fought against the Ottoman Empire; ANZAC (Australian and New Zealand) forces landed at ANZAC Cove and fought at Lone Pine and the Nek, inspiring Peter Weir's 1981 film Gallipoli
examples: what war included the Gallipoli Campaign against the Ottoman Empire, one of the Central Powers?
During this war, the "Light Horse" fought the Senussi and served in the battles of Lone Pine and the Nek, which inspired a 1981 Peter Weir film.

clues.clue: Anzac Day (April 25) commemorates this war's Gallipoli landings with traditions like a "gunfire breakfast" and golden syrup-laced oat biscuits; Maori troops also fought in the Gallipoli campaign
examples: A "gunfire breakfast" and golden syrup-laced oat biscuits are served on an April 25th holiday that honors this war's supposed "larrikin" forces.
Ngata is credited with boosting Maori commitment to this war, helping him advocate for Maori equality with Pakeha. Maori troops fought in this war's Gallipoli campaign.

clues.clue: T.E. Lawrence sponsored the Arab Revolt, which began after Sherif Hussein and Prince Faisal revolted against the forces of Enver Pasha and ended Ottoman control of the Middle East; it culminated in the capture of Damascus
examples: E. Lawrence sponsored an Arab Revolt that ended Ottoman control of the Middle East.
The capture of Damascus was the culmination of a revolt during this conflict which began after Sherif Hussein and Prince Faisal revolted against the forces of Enver Pasha.

clues.clue: General Edmund Allenby won a decisive victory at the Battle of Megiddo on the Nablus Plain, while General Henry Chauvel's charge helped capture Beersheba; the contested Hejaz Railway led to the Battle of Aqaba
examples: A battle on the Nablus Plain at Megiddo was a decisive victory for general Edmund Allenby during this conflict.
Henry Chauvel's charge helped ensure the capture of Beersheba during this conflict.

works.name: The East African Campaign
works.description: In German East Africa, General Paul von Lettow-Vorbeck led a small, mobile guerrilla force of African Askaris that tied down much larger British, French, Portuguese, and Belgian armies for the entire war, ultimately never being decisively defeated in the field even as Germany lost the colony.

clues.clue: Paul von Lettow-Vorbeck led a guerrilla force of Askaris that fought much larger groups of British, French, and Portuguese soldiers, ultimately resulting in the loss of the German East Africa colony
examples: During this war, Paul Emil von Lettow-Vorbeck led a guerilla force of Askaris that fought much larger groups of British, French, and Portuguese soldiers.
name this conflict which resulted in the loss of the German East Africa colony.

clues.clue: An invasion led by Arthur Aitken at the Battle of Tanga failed despite a 5-to-1 numerical advantage after some soldiers were swarmed by bees; mostly ethnically Indian forces were also defeated at Tanga and Mahiwa
examples: In a battle during this war, an invasion led by Arthur Aitken failed despite a 5 to 1 numerical advantage after some soldiers was swarmed by bees.
During this war, mostly ethnically Indian forces were defeated at Tanga and Mahiwa.

clues.clue: A general seeking supplies crossed the Ruvuma River and decisively won the Battle of Ngomano; Charles Crewe and Charles Tombeur each led allied armies in the coordinated Tabora Offensive
examples: A general seeking supplies during this war crossed the Ruvuma River and decisively won the Battle of Ngomano.
During this war, Charles Crewe and Charles Tombeur each led two allied armies in co-ordinated parallel invasions in the successful Tabora Offensive.

clues.clue: American-educated pastor John Chilembwe led a revolt during this war
examples: The revolt of American-educated pastor John Chilembwe occurred during this war.

works.name: The War at Sea
works.description: Britain's Royal Navy blockaded Germany's ports for the war's duration, while Germany responded with unrestricted submarine warfare, most notoriously in the sinking of the RMS Lusitania. The blockade and the Lusitania sinking are each clued through their severe human cost and their unusually deep imprint on classical music and popular song.

clues.clue: The British Royal Navy's blockade of Germany was enforced by the Northern and Dover Patrols and remained in force eight months after the November armistice, only ending with the signing of the Treaty of Versailles; historian A.C. Bell estimated it caused seven hundred thousand deaths
examples: It was still in force eight months after the November armistice, only ending with the signing of the Treaty of Versailles.
C. Bell estimated that it caused seven hundred thousand deaths.

clues.clue: Germany introduced kriegsbrot ("war bread") in response to the blockade, which Britain was able to legally justify within an 800-mile range under the Second Hague Conference
examples: One side started using kriegsbrot during this campaign, which was legally justified to be prosecuted within an 800 mile range by the Second Hague Conference.

clues.clue: A German U-boat sank the British ocean liner RMS Lusitania in 1915, an event that inspired Charles Ives's Orchestral Set No. 2 ("From Hanover Square North") and Frank Bridge's "Lament for Catherine, aged 9"
examples: name this 1915 event in which a German U-boat destroyed a British ocean liner.
"Sweet By and By" emerges from chaos in his piece about this event in Orchestral Set No. 2, From Hanover Square North.

clues.clue: This war's naval war included the Battle of Jutland
examples: name this conflict including the Battle of Jutland, during which the Scandinavian countries refused to join either the Allied or Central Powers.

works.name: United States Entry and Mobilization
works.description: The United States entered the war in 1917 after campaigning on neutrality, driven by the Zimmermann Telegram and the sinking of the Lusitania. Its entry was preceded by the "preparedness movement" and accompanied by an extensive propaganda and conscription apparatus that reshaped American civil life.

clues.clue: Room 40 decoded the Zimmermann Telegram, a German communique to Venustiano Carranza's government offering Mexico the return of "lost territory" in Texas in exchange for making war and peace together against the U.S.
examples: A different communique in this war was decoded by Room 40 and offered to "make war and peace together." That note, sent to the government of Venustiano Carranza, also offered, "lost territory" in Texas
name this war, during which Zimmerman Telegram was received, fought between the Central Powers and the Allies.

clues.clue: The U.S. entered the war in 1917, breaking Woodrow Wilson's re-election slogan "He kept us out of the war"
examples: name this goal, which broke Woodrow Wilson's campaign slogan: "He kept us out of the war."
A year before the U.S. entered this conflict, the sitting President won re-election using a slogan of "He Kept Us Out Of War."

clues.clue: The Preparedness movement, led by figures including Theodore Roosevelt and Leonard Wood, promoted the "Plattsburg Idea" of civilian military training camps, gave rise to ROTC, and drove passage of the National Defense Act of 1916
examples: This movement, whose leaders produced the Plattsburg Idea, gave rise to the ROTC program.
Agitation from this movement led to the passage of the National Defense Act of 1916.

clues.clue: George Creel headed the Committee on Public Information (Creel Committee), which authorized "Four Minute Men" to give speeches and used propaganda posters, including Harry Hopps's image of Germany as a crazed gorilla, to encourage home-front support
examples: The Creel Committee used propaganda to encourage home front support for this war.
Harry Hopps created a World War I propaganda poster depicting Germany as a crazed gorilla holding a half-nude woman and a club

clues.clue: Conscription was upheld by the Supreme Court in Arver v. US; it mobilized the American Expeditionary Force's "Doughboys," some of whom were sent to Arkhangelsk in the Polar Bear Expedition to rescue the Czech Legion, while others (including the all-Black unit later defended by lawyer Turner Bell) fought in the Houston race riot
examples: The law establishing this policy was upheld by the Supreme Court in Arver v. US, which cited Emer de Vattel's Law of Nations.
A portion of the larger group created by this policy was sent to Arkhangelsk to rescue the Czech Legion in the Polar Bear Expedition.

clues.clue: Anti-war activists including Emma Goldman were prosecuted or deported under wartime laws; Schenck v. US and Abrams v. US, both centering on the distribution of anti-war pamphlets, reached the Supreme Court
examples: Emma Goldman was deported for opposing -what policy that mobilized Doughboys for the American Expeditionary Force in 1917?
Several World War I-era Supreme Court cases, including Schenck v. US and Abrams V. US, centered on the distribution of anti-war pamphlets.

works.name: Conscription and Political Divisions in the British Dominions
works.description: Conscription for this war split the domestic politics of several British dominions along ethnic and party lines: it broke apart Australia's Labor Party, triggered a full-blown constitutional crisis in Canada, and was resisted by Maori leaders in New Zealand who tied the issue to prior grievances against the Crown.

clues.clue: In Canada, Prime Minister Robert Borden's Unionist government passed conscription and won the "khaki election"; Wilfrid Laurier's Liberals opposed it, and Quebec nationalist Henri Bourassa led the anti-conscription movement in the Conscription Crisis of 1917
examples: Name this policy enacted by Robert Borden, whose Unionist faction won a "khaki" election due to two new laws passed supporting it.
A politician with this last name led Quebec's anti-conscription movement and earlier founded the Ligue nationaliste.

clues.clue: Billy Hughes's proposal to conscript soldiers for this war split Australia's Labor Party
examples: Billy Hughes's proposal to conscript soldiers for this war split the Labor Party.
Later in life, Miller campaigned against conscription for this conflict, which led to a split in the labor party under Billy Hughes.

clues.clue: In New Zealand, local Maori men circumvented conscription with the help of Princess Te Puea, who opposed British conscription because she believed Maori should not fight for a Crown responsible for the Treaty of Waitangi
examples: Name this policy that local men circumvented with the help of Princess Te Puea.
Te Puea opposed British conscription during World War I, believing that members of this ethnic group should not fight for a British crown responsible for the Treaty of Waitangi.

works.name: Women, Suffrage, and the Home Front
works.description: This war reshaped the roles available to women, who took on new industrial and military support jobs and used the war to advance or oppose the cause of suffrage. It also produced a distinctive home-front culture of shame-based recruitment and international protest.

clues.clue: This war led to British women gaining the right to vote; suffragette Emmeline Pankhurst supported the war effort and popularized handing out white feathers, while her daughter Sylvia Pankhurst opposed the war, causing a rift between mother and daughter
examples: name this war that led to women gaining the right to vote in Britain.
A disagreement over support for this war caused a rift between mother and daughter Emmeline and Sylvia Pankhurst.

clues.clue: Women who served on naval vessels were nicknamed "Wrens," beginning in this war; Josephine Carr became the first Wren to die in it when her ship was sunk; "Canary girls" were women whose skin turned yellow from working with TNT in munitions factories
examples: Beginning in this war, women who served on naval vessels were nicknamed "Wrens"; Josephine Carr became the first Wren to die in this war when her ship was sunk.
"Canary girls" were women whose skin turned yellow as a result of working with TNT to make munitions during this war.

clues.clue: The International Congress of Women at the Hague, chaired by Jane Addams, was convened to protest this war
examples: The International Congress of Women at the Hague, chaired by Jane Addams, was convened to protest this war.

works.name: Literature of the War
works.description: This war produced an unusually large canon of soldier-authored literature, ranging from British "war poets" (Owen, Sassoon, Graves) treated in the general identifiers above, to prose accounts by veterans on both sides that range from bitter anti-war realism to comic satire.

clues.clue: Erich Maria Remarque's All Quiet on the Western Front follows a German soldier and his classmate Kemmerich, who are encouraged to enlist by their teacher Kantorek
examples: In another novel, the protagonist and his classmate Kemmerich are encouraged to take part in this event by Kantorek.
name this conflict described in Under Fire, The Good Soldier Svejk, and All Quiet on the Western Front.

clues.clue: Henri Barbusse's Under Fire won the Prix Goncourt; Jaroslav Hasek's The Good Soldier Svejk follows an absurdly patriotic protagonist arrested for treason and sent to a madhouse before the war
examples: Henri Barbusse won a Prix Goncourt for his novel about this event
The protagonist of that novel is so absurdly patriotic that he is arrested for treason before being sent to a madhouse

clues.clue: Ernest Hemingway set A Farewell to Arms in this war, in which the narrator's love interest is Catherine Barkley and Jake Barnes (of The Sun Also Rises) received his wound
examples: name this war, during which Hemingway set A Farewell to Arms.
In another novel, Jake Barnes received the wound that rendered him impotent in this war.

clues.clue: Dalton Trumbo's Johnny Got His Gun and John Dos Passos's Three Soldiers are set during this war; David Jones's long poem In Parenthesis is also set during it
examples: Dalton Trumbo's Johnny Got his Gun and John Dos Passos's Three Soldiers are set during this war.
David Jones's "In Parenthesis" is set during this event

clues.clue: Pat Barker's Regeneration Trilogy (Regeneration, The Eye in the Door, The Ghost Road) fictionalizes Siegfried Sassoon's treatment by psychologist W.H.R. Rivers at Craiglockhart Hospital, where Owen and Sassoon met
examples: The Ghost Road and Regeneration are Pat Barker novels set during this period that fictionalize the author of The Complete Memoirs of George Sherston.
Owen and Sassoon met in a hospital while serving in this war

clues.clue: Vera Brittain's memoir Testament of Youth describes her service in the Voluntary Aid Detachment and her romance with Roland Leighton, who died in this war
examples: Name this memoir that describes the author's time working in the Voluntary Aid Detachment.
A romance with Richard Leighton during the height of this conflict is discussed in Vera Brittain's Testament of Youth.

clues.clue: Ernst Junger's memoir Storm of Steel describes being shot through the chest during the Spring Offensive; Junger also wrote the novel On the Marble Cliffs
examples: Junger's Storm of Steel is set during this conflict that also provides the setting of All Quiet on the Western Front.
This author wrote about being shot through the chest while acting as an assault troop during the spring offensive in a best-selling 1920 memoir.

clues.clue: Robert Graves's memoir Goodbye to All That describes meeting Owen and Sassoon during this war; Ford Madox Ford's Parade's End novels follow soldier Christopher Tietjens during it
examples: He wrote about meeting Owen and Sassoon during World War I in his memoir Goodbye to All That.
Ford Madox Ford's Parade's End novels are about Christopher Tietjens, who serves as a soldier in this war.

clues.clue: Rebecca West's novel The Return of the Soldier centers on shell-shocked veteran Chris Baldry, who loses his memory of his wife Kitty
examples: Kitty is emotionally crushed by the memory loss suffered by her shell-shocked husband Chris Baldry in The Return of the Soldier, a World War I novel

clues.clue: A veteran of this war spends time in a park with his wife Lucrezia before committing suicide in Virginia Woolf's Mrs. Dalloway
examples: A veteran of this conflict spends some time in a park with his wife Lucrezia before committing suicide in Mrs. Dalloway.

works.name: Art of the War
works.description: This war produced a distinctive visual art movement of trauma and disillusionment, ranging from Britain's officially commissioned battlefield paintings to the savage etchings and paintings of German veterans, and it directly precipitated the founding of the Dada movement.

clues.clue: John Singer Sargent's painting Gassed depicts a line of blindfolded, wounded soldiers walking toward a dressing station; it was commissioned by the Britain War Memorials Committee
examples: A line of wounded soldiers walk toward a dressing station in a painting of this war by John Singer Sargent.
this war is the setting of Sargent's Gassed and Dix's The Trench.

clues.clue: Otto Dix depicted his experiences in the war in the etching series Der Krieg and the painting The Trench, and later painted a veteran with two wooden legs into his Metropolis triptych
examples: A cycle of 51 prints about this war was created by an artist who depicted a veteran of this war with two wooden legs in his Metropolis triptych, Otto Dix.
The etching series Der Krieg depicts Otto Dix's experiences fighting in this war.

clues.clue: Christopher Nevinson's painting Paths of Glory, depicting dead soldiers on barbed wire, was publicized by covering it with a banner reading "censored"
examples: By putting a banner with the word "censored" over a piece, Christopher Nevinson publicized his sardonic painting of this war titled Paths of Glory.

clues.clue: Kathe Kollwitz's sculpture Mother with Her Dead Son reflects her grief after her son Peter died in this war; her print series The Weavers and The Peasant War also depict the effects of war
examples: Kollwitz's sculpture Mother with her Dead Son reflects her depression after her son Peter died in this war.
Her series of etchings and lithographs depicting the effects of war include The Weavers and The Peasant War.

clues.clue: The New Objectivity movement developed after this war, the Vorticist movement broke up partly as a result of it, and despair over it prompted the founding of Dadaism
examples: After this war, the New Objectivity movement of George Grosz developed
Despair over this war prompted the foundation of Dadaism.

works.name: Armistice, the Treaty of Versailles, and Legacy
works.description: The war ended with an armistice in November 1918 and was formally closed by the Treaty of Versailles, whose harsh reparations regime shaped interwar Germany's politics and economy for over a decade. The war's memory was also politically weaponized, most consequentially through the German "stab-in-the-back" myth.

clues.clue: Article 231 of the Treaty of Versailles, usually called the "war guilt clause," formed the basis for reparations; these were later restructured by the Dawes Plan and Young Plan, and formally suspended at a 1932 conference in Lausanne
examples: The case for reparations was outlined in Article 231 of the Treaty of Versailles, which is usually known by this name
Identify these very long-term payments whose restructuring was the subject of the Dawes and Young Plans.

clues.clue: The Treaty of Lausanne (1923), which recognized the state of Turkey, was the final treaty formally ending this war
examples: A 1923 treaty here recognized the state of Turkey and was the final treaty ending World War I.

clues.clue: Germany's hyperinflation crisis, which saw the reissuance of notgeld emergency currency, was fueled in part by reparations imposed under the Treaty of Versailles; the 1923 Occupation of the Ruhr was justified by Germany's failure to deliver reparations goods
examples: One cause of Germany's hyperinflation were these entities imposed on the country through the Treaty of Versailles
The Occupation of the Ruhr in 1923 was justified by Germany failing to make a delivery of this good to the Allies.

clues.clue: The myth that Germany lost this war due to internal betrayal, blaming "November criminals," is known as the "stab-in-the-back" legend (Dolchstoss), promulgated by Erich Ludendorff, which fueled the growth of anti-Semitism under the Weimar Republic
examples: The myth that a country lost this war because of internal strife is known as the "stabbed in the back" myth.
"November criminals" were blamed for defeat in this war by the "stab-in-the-back legend," which fueled the growth of anti-Semitism under the Weimar Republic.

clues.clue: The Bezprizorni were homeless children left in the war's aftermath, aided by an organization founded by Anatoly Lunacharsky
examples: The Bezprizorni were a group of homeless children left in the aftermath of this war, and they were aided by an organization founded after this war by Anatoly Lunacharsky.

comprehensive_summary: This war began in the summer of 1914 when the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo triggered the collapse of Europe's alliance system: the Triple Entente of Britain, France, and Russia mobilized against the Central Powers after Germany's Schlieffen Plan violated Belgian neutrality, an agreement Britain derided as a "scrap of paper." Historians from A.J.P. Taylor to Barbara Tuchman, Fritz Fischer, Christopher Clark, and Arno Mayer have long disputed how much blame belongs to German aggression, alliance mechanics, or domestic politics. On the Western Front, the German advance was checked at the First Battle of the Marne and gave way to trench deadlock, punctuated by the Christmas Truce of 1914, the Somme, Vimy Ridge, and Passchendaele, where the Canadian Corps distinguished itself. The Eastern Front opened with Russian disasters at Tannenberg and the Masurian Lakes, saw the costly Brusilov Offensive and the siege of Przemysl, and ended with Russia's exit via the Treaty of Brest-Litovsk amid the Russian Revolution. Beyond Europe, the failed Gallipoli Campaign against the Ottomans shaped Australian and New Zealand national memory (Anzac Day), while T.E. Lawrence's Arab Revolt, culminating in the capture of Damascus, ended Ottoman rule in the Middle East; in German East Africa, Paul von Lettow-Vorbeck's guerrilla Askari force tied down vastly larger Allied armies for the war's duration. At sea, Britain's blockade of Germany (enforced by the Northern and Dover Patrols) and Germany's submarine warfare, most infamously the 1915 sinking of the RMS Lusitania, inspired works from Charles Ives to Frank Bridge. The United States, having campaigned on neutrality under Woodrow Wilson, entered in 1917 after the Zimmermann Telegram and the Lusitania sinking, mobilizing through conscription (upheld in Arver v. US), George Creel's propaganda apparatus, and a Preparedness movement rooted in the Plattsburg Idea. Conscription also convulsed the British dominions, splitting Australia's Labor Party under Billy Hughes, provoking Canada's Conscription Crisis between Robert Borden and Henri Bourassa, and drawing Maori resistance under Princess Te Puea in New Zealand. On the home front, women's wartime labor and activism, including the Pankhursts' divided loyalties, helped win British women the vote. The war produced an enormous literary canon, from the war poetry of Owen and Sassoon to prose by Remarque, Barbusse, Hasek, Hemingway, Brittain, Jünger, and Graves, and a parallel visual canon led by Sargent, Dix, Nevinson, and Kollwitz, whose disillusionment helped spawn Dada and New Objectivity. The war ended with the November 1918 armistice and the Treaty of Versailles, whose Article 231 "war guilt" clause and reparations regime (later restructured by the Dawes and Young Plans) fed both German hyperinflation and the poisonous "stab-in-the-back" myth that fueled Weimar-era anti-Semitism; formal peace with Ottoman successor Turkey was not concluded until the 1923 Treaty of Lausanne.
recursive_suggestions: Fetch results for 'World War I' pulled in a large amount of unrelated content because 'World War I' is a literal substring of 'World War II' -- 18 tossups and dozens of bonus parts whose actual answer was 'World War II' (or Holocaust-adjacent topics) were excluded from this page; future re-fetches of this topic should account for this substring-matching quirk.
World War II -- a separate, extremely well-attested topic; excluded here entirely (18 tossups, dozens of bonus parts)
The Holocaust -- separate topic, excluded (multiple tossup answerlines: 'the Holocaust'/'The Holocaust')

Battle of the Somme / Battle of Vimy Ridge / Battle of Passchendaele / Battle of the Marne / Battle of Jutland -- each recurs as a named battle within World War I clues but never appeared as its own answerline in this fetch; candidates for standalone History pages if they accumulate independent clue density
Gallipoli Campaign -- densely clued sub-theater with its own cultural legacy (Anzac Day); could support a standalone page
sinking of the RMS Lusitania -- appeared as its own bonus-part answerline (not just a WWI mention) with a distinct cluster of classical-music clues; candidate for a standalone Event page
The United Kingdom's Naval Blockade of Germany in World War I -- appeared as its own bonus-part answerline; candidate for a standalone Event page
Zimmermann Telegram -- recurring device with independent clue potential (Room 40 decryption, Carranza's Mexico)
T.E. Lawrence / the Arab Revolt -- recurring figure and campaign; candidate for a standalone Figure or Event page
Paul von Lettow-Vorbeck -- recurring East African campaign figure with independent clue density
Treaty of Versailles -- recurring document with its own clue potential (war guilt clause, reparations, Dawes/Young Plans); candidate for a standalone Document page
Treaty of Brest-Litovsk -- recurring document tied to Russia's exit from the war; candidate for a standalone page
Wilfred Owen / Siegfried Sassoon -- both are dense recurring Literature figures with independent clue potential beyond their role as WWI identifiers
All Quiet on the Western Front (Erich Maria Remarque) -- recurring Literature work with independent clue density

Second Sino-Japanese War, Operation Barbarossa, German invasion of the Soviet Union, German occupation/invasion of France, Allied/American Occupation of Japan, Japanese American internment, V-J Day, atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki -- all separate World War II-era topics, excluded

Ambiguity flag: many singleton bonus-part clues in the fetched corpus referenced other wars in passing (Russo-Japanese War, Boer War, WWII Pacific theater) via shared authors or motifs (e.g., Roald Dahl, Robert Capa, Rebecca West's later novels); these were excluded as belonging to different topics.
